title: Battle of Marathon
tagline: 10,000 Athenians. 25,000 Persians. No cavalry. No allies. No choice.
hook_line: The Persian army has 25,000 soldiers on the beach at Marathon. Athens has 10,000. The Spartans said they'd help — but they can't come for six days. The battle is happening now.

scenes.title: The Beach at Marathon
scenes.narration: 490 BC. The Persian Empire has sent 600 ships and 25,000 soldiers to the beach at Marathon, 26 miles from Athens. Their goal: destroy the city that had helped burn Sardis, and make an example of Greek defiance for the entire world to see. The Athenian army of 10,000 men has been watching them from the hills for five days. They have no cavalry. They have no navy. The Spartans sent word: we cannot march for six more days. Our religious festival is not finished.
scenes.visual_prompt: Epic aerial establishing shot of the bay of Marathon — the enormous Persian fleet of 600 ships crowding the coastline, tents and encampments of 25,000 soldiers stretching across the flat plain, Persian banners in deep crimson and gold, beyond the plains — hills where tiny Athenian figures are visible watching from above, atmosphere: late morning Mediterranean light, the scale of the Persian force genuinely overwhelming against the landscape, cinematic historical epic style, warm amber and deep crimson color palette, #8B1A1A Persian red and #C9A227 gold, #2C1810 dark background

scenes.title: What Darius Knows
scenes.narration: The Persian king Darius I had conquered Egypt, Babylon, and the Indus Valley. He had built the greatest empire the world had ever seen. When his advisors told him Athens had sent 10,000 men to oppose his 25,000, he is said to have asked: 'Who are the Athenians?' He was not being dismissive. He genuinely did not know. They were that small.
scenes.visual_prompt: Dramatic portrait composition of Darius I — Persian king in full royal regalia, gold crown, deep crimson and blue robes, massive throne in his command tent, Persian imperial seal visible, campaign maps of conquered territories visible in background — Egypt, Babylon, India all marked — he's examining a small map of Attica, the entire territory of Athens visible as a tiny region, advisors around him, the visual language communicating genuine imperial supremacy, #2C1810 and #C9A227, regal and overwhelming

scenes.title: Miltiades Makes the Case
scenes.narration: The Athenian generals held a war council. Five voted to wait for the Spartans. Five voted to attack now. The deciding vote belonged to the war archon, Callimachus. General Miltiades made the case: 'If we wait, our nerve breaks. The people of Athens lose hope. Some will open the gates. If we fight now — outnumbered, in the open — we will fight with everything we have, because everything is at stake. Men fight differently when they have no other option.'
scenes.visual_prompt: Tense war council scene — Athenian generals in bronze armor and red cloaks around a camp table, Miltiades standing making his case, Callimachus the war archon seated at center listening, the vote tally visible on a simple tablet — 5 and 5 with one space remaining, a small figurine of Athena visible on the table (Athenian generals carried these), the Persian camp fires visible through the tent opening behind them, dramatic side lighting, #2C1810 and #8B4513 earthy tones, genuine tension in the composition

scenes.title: The Problem of Persian Arrows
scenes.narration: There was one thing everyone knew about Persian armies: the archers. Before any engagement, Persian archers would darken the sky with arrows. The Greek soldier Dieneces, upon hearing that Persian arrows would block out the sun, said: 'Good. Then we will fight in the shade.' But that was bravado. Arrows are not shade. The Greeks had to cross 1,500 meters of open ground before they could close with Persian infantry — 1,500 meters of arrow fire. Unless they ran it.
scenes.visual_prompt: Visual split showing the problem: left half — dramatic visualization of Persian archers in mass formation, thousands of bows raised, arrows covering the sky in a darkening wave, the sheer volume making the sky visible underneath look like shade — right half — Greek soldiers in bronze panoply (heavy armor, shields, spears) shown having to cross 1,500 meters of flat open ground, the distance marked, vulnerability clear, the tactical problem communicated visually without text, #2C1810 and #C9A227, historical epic cinematic style

scenes.title: The Decision: Run
scenes.narration: Miltiades made a plan that had never been tried before. The Greek center would be deliberately thin — weak enough to draw the Persian center forward. The flanks would be strong. When the Persian center pushed through the middle, the Greek flanks would fold inward around them. And to get across the arrow kill zone — they would run. 10,000 men in full bronze armor would run 1,500 meters into the Persian army. The Persians would not believe it. Arrows are aimed at standing targets.
scenes.visual_prompt: Top-down tactical map view of the Marathon plain — Greek line visible at one end, thinned deliberately at center, wings thickened — Persian line visible at the other end, their mass formation showing strength concentrated at center and cavalry on flanks — red arrows showing the Persian expected center push — blue arrows showing the Greek double-envelopment — and a single dramatic visual device: all 10,000 Greek figures shown beginning to run toward the Persian line, the motion blur of a full army in charge, cinematic overhead tactical graphic style, #2C1810 background, #C9A227 and #4169E1 tactical lines

scenes.title: The Charge
scenes.narration: When the Persian army saw 10,000 armored Greeks begin to run toward them, they thought they had gone mad. Then the arrows rose. But Greeks at a sprint cover ground differently than Greeks at a march. Some fell. But the line did not break. When the bronze shields hit the Persian infantry, the sound was heard three miles away. The Persian center pushed through — exactly as planned. The Greek flanks folded inward. The most powerful army in the world was surrounded on a beach by men who had been given no other choice.
scenes.visual_prompt: Epic battle scene — the moment of Greek shield wall impact with Persian infantry at the Marathon beach, dramatic composition: Greek bronze shields and crimson cloaks crashing into Persian infantry in deep color, Persian archers visible at rear no longer able to shoot into the melee, the double envelopment beginning — Greek wings visible curling inward on both sides — Persian soldiers beginning to be compressed toward the sea, the scale showing 10,000 Greeks against 25,000 Persians but the Persians now trapped, cinematic battle epic style, #2C1810 and #8B1A1A with #C9A227 bronze glinting, dust and motion

scenes.title: The Run to Athens
scenes.narration: 6,400 Persians died at Marathon. 192 Athenians died. The Persian fleet, still intact, turned south — they were going directly to Athens to attack while the army was away. Miltiades marched his army back to Athens immediately. They covered 26 miles and arrived before the Persian fleet. The Persians saw the victorious army waiting on the shore and turned back to sea. The battle was over. A soldier named Pheidippides ran from Marathon to Athens to deliver the news. He arrived, said one word — 'Nike' — victory — and died. That run has been repeated every year for 2,500 years.
scenes.visual_prompt: Dual composition: top — the battered but standing Athenian army on the shore of Athens Bay watching the Persian fleet turn away — 600 ships that came to destroy a city now retreating, the contrast between the fleet's size and its retreat — bottom — the solitary figure of Pheidippides running alone, full bronze armor removed, simple tunic, the 26-mile road to Athens visible, the city in the background, the word NIKE in ancient Greek script appearing as he arrives, the weight and cost in his posture, cinematic historical, warm golden Mediterranean light, #C9A227 and #2C1810

scenes.title: What It Changed
scenes.narration: Marathon changed the world's understanding of what Greece was. Before Marathon, Persia was the center of the known world and Greece was a collection of quarreling city-states on the edge of it. After Marathon, the Greeks knew something that had not been known before: the Persian army could be beaten. The idea that became Western civilization was born not in a philosophy class but on a beach with shields, at a sprint, against a force three times its size.
scenes.visual_prompt: Sweeping historical consequence visualization — before/after split: left panel shows the Pre-Marathon world map — Persian Empire vast and dominant, colored in deep crimson covering Egypt, Mesopotamia, Persia, the edge of India, Greece shown as a tiny cluster at the western margin — right panel shows the Post-Marathon era — the birth of the Classical Greek period marked, Athens marked with a golden point, the future paths of Greek influence shown expanding — philosophy, democracy, architecture — the contrast between Greece's size and its eventual influence, cinematic historical infographic style, #2C1810 and #C9A227

scenes.title: What Darius Did Next
scenes.narration: Darius I did not accept the defeat. He began immediately planning a second invasion — larger, total, designed to leave nothing standing. He died before he could launch it. His son Xerxes inherited both the throne and the mission. The second Persian invasion — 300,000 soldiers, 1,200 ships — came ten years later. Thermopylae. Salamis. The world was not finished with this conflict.
scenes.visual_prompt: Portrait of Darius I in his tent after the news of Marathon, the crimson banner still dominant, Persian power still visibly immense — but his expression is different now: not surprise but cold resolution, a scribe visible writing the order for a second invasion, a map of Greece with Marathon circled being replaced by a map showing a much larger invasion force, the Persian empire still in every way superior in scale, the visual communicating: this is not over, this is just the beginning, #2C1810 and #8B1A1A, ominous and powerful

scenes.title: The Run That Lasted 2,500 Years
scenes.narration: Every year, in cities around the world, millions of people run 26.2 miles. They call it a marathon. They are running the same distance Pheidippides ran to bring word of the battle. Most of them do not die at the finish line. Pheidippides is why the distance exists at all. He ran so that Athens would know it was still alive. Athens was still alive. So, for a few more decades at least, was Greece.
scenes.visual_prompt: Split timeline visualization — left: the ancient road from Marathon to Athens, Pheidippides alone running in morning light, the ancient Mediterranean landscape — right: modern marathon runners in a city race, thousands of modern participants, city skyline, a modern finish line — the same distance, 2,500 years apart — a single connecting element: the number 26 miles visible in both frames — the ancient and modern running the same road for the same reason, warm light, cinematic historical-to-modern bridge composition, #C9A227 and #2C1810 to modern palette

viral_hook: 10,000 Athenians ran in full armor across 1,500 meters of open ground toward 25,000 Persians — because the arrows are aimed at people standing still. It worked. And the distance they ran is why you call it a marathon.
highlight_note: The tactical decision to run — the visual of 10,000 armored men charging from above — and the reveal that arrows are aimed at standing targets. Rewatch immediately.
short_title: Why You Call It A Marathon
rewatch_bait: Scene 4 — Dieneces's quote about fighting in the shade. It's bravado in the scene. But in scene 6, when the arrows rise and the Greeks are already past them — he was right.
youtube_title: 10,000 vs 25,000 — and they decided to RUN at them 🏃 | Gods & Glory S2 EP1
lesson: When you have no other option, the impossible becomes the only plan. Athens didn't win at Marathon despite being outnumbered — they won because they ran when no one expected running.
arc_notes: GG S2 OPEN. Marathon sets the template for GG S2: outnumbered forces winning through unconventional tactics. Connects forward to Thermopylae and Salamis (the second Persian invasion Darius ordered). The marathon run origin story is the rewatch/share hook. Villain mandate maintained: Darius feels genuinely dominant — 600 ships, conquered Egypt and Babylon — his dismissal of Athens is legitimate, not arrogance.
